US Macro Forecast

B · Track recordTrack record
The model ↗

A regularly updated forecast for the US economy from a 32-variable Bayesian VAR: a statistical model that learns the joint dynamics of the main US macroeconomic series from their history and projects them forward as a full distribution around each path. It is estimated on quarterly public data back to 1986 and re-run each vintage. This page is the forecast itself — the headline paths, the current-quarter nowcast and the five-year view, and how the forecasts have held up out of sample. How the model works, and the structural analysis behind these numbers, is on The model.

AThe forecastthe headline paths, the four forecasts, the numbers, the current quarter and the five-year view

The four forecasts, explained
One model, four disciplines: what each published path assumes. The current-quarter nowcast additionally folds partial monthly data into the opening quarter. Where all four agree the call is model-robust; where they fan apart, the gap shows which assumption is doing the work.
Model baselineNothing imposed — the model’s own view.
Theory-anchoredLong-run economic relationships built into the prior.
Benchmark-anchoredReweighted toward public long-run benchmarks.
Analyst-anchoredNear-term pinned to a central analyst path.
See the schematic for each forecast, and a worked example
Model baseline
Theory-anchored
Benchmark-anchored
Analyst-anchored
One worked example — the same path, judged four ways
Take the term spread: the 10-year yield minus the fed funds rate, which has averaged about 1.3pp since 1986. Suppose one simulated future lets the 10-year yield climb to 6% while fed funds settles at 3%, a 3pp gap that keeps widening. Model baseline has no opinion: nothing in its prior connects where the two rates end up. Theory-anchored objects at estimation time. Its prior treats a permanently widening spread as unlikely before any forecast is drawn, so coefficients that generate such paths carry less weight. Note what it does not do: it never says where either rate should be, only that the gap between them should not trend. Benchmark-anchored would not notice the spread at all, but would down-weight the same path afterwards if its five-year GDP growth strayed from 1.9%. Analyst-anchored would have pinned both rates directly for the first two years, leaving the far end to the model plus its annual tilts. Same model, same data: the four forecasts are these four judgements applied to every simulated path.

Nowcast — 2026Q2

The model's current-quarter real GDP read, and how the monthly data released so far have moved it. Beta.
2.8%q/q annualised · Nowcast
Current-quarter (2026Q2) real GDP · Model baseline 1.9%
The Nowcast folds the partial monthly data already released for the current quarter into the model (a mixed-frequency BVAR, reweighted by entropic tilting, Krüger-Clark-Ravazzolo 2017); it is the primary current-quarter read. It is a side output — the headline forecast is unchanged.
Annualised GDP growth by estimate
EstimateMedian (66% band)
Model baseline+1.94%+0.07 to +4.11%
Nowcast+2.79%+1.13 to +4.50%
Nowcast: 6 pooled chains, split-R̂ 1.033 (a sampler-convergence check; ∼1.0 is healthy). Fold-in: effective sample-size ratio 0.90, mean-only (nowcast/forecast variance ratio 0.43).
How the partial monthly data move the forecast
The Model baseline re-run with the monthly indicators already released for the current quarter (the nowcast, red), against the path that ignores them (grey). The marked points on each chart are the current forecast quarter, both values labelled.
hover or tap to flip the bands · tap again to unpin · default: Nowcast
What moved the GDP nowcast
Each released indicator is compared with what the Model baseline expected for it; the gap (the “news”) propagates through the model's dynamics to a ragged-edge current-quarter (2026Q2) GDP estimate. The bar and the last table column are that propagated contribution, in percentage points of q/q annualised GDP growth, and they sum to the net news. This is a diagnostic of how each release moves the ragged-edge read; it is computed separately from the headline mixed-frequency nowcast above, so the two need not sum to the same number. The nowcast draws on monthly data across 2 partial quarters (2026Q2, 2026Q3); where a series printed in more than one, the table shows its largest surprise and its total contribution.

Housing Starts% y/y-0.75.0-5.7-0.09
Cleveland Fed 10-yr Inflation Expectations%2.52.3+0.2-0.09
Federal funds rate%3.63.4+0.2+0.09
10-year Treasury yield%4.44.2+0.2+0.07
Moody's Aaa%5.55.3+0.2-0.06
Economic Policy Uncertainty Index% y/y-48.3-31.6-16.7+0.06
TR1y Rate%3.83.5+0.3+0.06
Unemployment rate%4.34.4-0.2+0.06
Potential GDP% y/y2.22.2-0.0-0.05
Net news-0.09
Released and Model-expected are each series' own basis — year-on-year % for activity and prices, level % for interest rates. Surprise = released − expected; a bold surprise exceeds two standard deviations of the model's predictive distribution (well beyond what the model expected before the release).

The long-run view

Where each forecast object takes the economy over the next five years, and whether the long-run relationships hold together. Hover or tap a chip to flip that forecast's nested 66/90% bands. Coherence checks, not accuracy claims.
This module tracks where each published forecast takes the economy over the next five years — far enough out that the paths reflect each forecast's steady state rather than the news, and the horizon of the growth densities below — and whether the long-run relationships that should hold the system together actually do. Four forecasts appear, named by where their discipline comes from. Model baseline is the model's own forecast under the standard statistical prior, with no outside information — the benchmark for what anchoring adds. Theory-anchored builds long-run discipline into the model itself, through a prior that holds economically meaningful relationships (spreads, expenditure shares) together rather than adjusting the output afterwards. Benchmark-anchored reweights the model's paths toward public long-run benchmarks (CBO potential growth and published long-run rates). Analyst-anchored is conditioned on the analyst baseline near term and anchored to its long-run views. The theory-anchored forecast is a cross-check whose role is fixed in advance. Long-run accuracy cannot be scored in real time at these horizons, so the diagnostics here are coherence checks against theory and public benchmarks, not accuracy claims.
hover or tap to flip the bands · tap again to unpin · default: Model baseline
How to read this chart
Each ridge is one forecast object’s full distribution of real GDP growth five years out (year-on-year, quarter 20); the dot marks its median and the dashed rule the CBO long-run benchmark. Read overlap as agreement between objects and width as genuine uncertainty. Densities are drawn over each forecast’s 1–99% range with a heavy-tail-robust bandwidth; the far tails are reported as the shaded 90% bands above and the band-width column in the numbers table below.
Coherence checks
How to read this chart
Each chart shows an economically meaningful combination of variables — a spread or a great ratio — with its history (black) and each prior's forecast of it. Theory says the spreads and the output gap are stationary: a coherent long-run forecast keeps them fluctuating around a stable level, and a line that walks away is the model quietly pulling the system apart (exactly where an unanchored levels VAR fails first). The labour and consumption shares are different: in the post-1986 data they trend, so a forecast that keeps trending is the model being right, not drifting — the subtitle on each panel says which reading applies. The comparison isolates the theory prior: the standard sum-of-coefficients prior versus the long-run-relationships prior on otherwise identical models. Watch the output-gap panel: the model baseline lets the gap drift over the forecast, while the theory-anchored forecast holds it near zero. That discipline, not any hard-coded number, is what the theory-anchored forecast contributes.
hover or tap to flip the bands · tap again to unpin · default: Model baseline
The numbers
The numbers behind the pictures. GDP drift is the annualised median growth rate over forecast years 6–10, past the charted window: the model's implied steady-state growth, read against CBO potential (≈1.9%). The pure model baseline runs hot, the benchmark-anchored forecast is pulled to the target by reweighting, and the theory-anchored forecast lands near it from inside the model. Fed Funds median @5y is where each object settles the policy rate at the charts' horizon. (The benchmark anchor binds on the target year's average, not on the terminal quarter shown here; because the far-horizon rate distribution is right-skewed, the displayed median can sit a little below the anchor even when the year's mean is on it.) Band width is the credible interval at year 5 — in-model discipline prices some extra far-horizon rate uncertainty, shown rather than hidden. Median max root measures system stability: levels models run deliberately close to 1; the long-run prior sits slightly higher, which is why its validation gate tracks drift and roots together.
Forecast objectGDP drift yrs 6–10 (CBO ≈ 1.9)Fed Funds median @5yFF 66% band width @5yGDP y/y 90% band width @5yMedian max root
Model baseline2.92 %/yr3.1%6.7pp7.7pp1.023
Theory-anchored2.00 %/yr3.8%7.0pp8.7pp1.048
Benchmark-anchored—2.7%7.1pp8.2pp—
Analyst-anchored—3.0%5.1pp6.9pp—
Model vs CBO long-run benchmarks
How to read this chart
Each forecast object in levels against the Congressional Budget Office’s published potential GDP and natural rate of unemployment, over the same five-year window. The benchmark-anchored path is the one steered toward these; the others show where the model goes on its own. A gap that widens against the benchmark is a deliberate model view, not an error — the point of the panel is to make that divergence visible.

What drove the economy — shock decomposition

The variance decomposition on the model page asks which shocks will drive forecast uncertainty; this asks which shocks did drive the data, back to the global financial crisis. Solid black line: the actual series. Dashed grey line: the model's trend path, where the series was heading on its own momentum from five years earlier with every shock switched off. For these growth and rate variables that is essentially the model's trend (potential growth, anchored inflation, the natural rate, the neutral yield); the bars show why the last five years deviated from it. Bars: the shock families' contributions plus the non-structural Covid 2020 component (means across 150 accepted identifications), which sum exactly from the baseline to the actual value at every date. The GDP and unemployment y-axes are set from the non-pandemic quarters so the 2020–21 swings don't compress everything else; those bars and the actual line run off-scale there, with their exact values on hover. Same shock families and colours as the variance decomposition. The same decomposition under the shadow model and its 9-shock menu is on the model page; agreement across the two is the cross-model robustness check. Note the two sit on different estimands (production: conditionally-uniform, RZ engine; shadow: joint-uniform accept-reject) — measured ≤1.2pp apart on headline shares, so read close agreement as robustness and small gaps as estimand, not economics. Hover for values. Why this stops at the present. A natural next question is whether the same bars can be carried forward to show what is driving the projection. They cannot be, and it is not an oversight. The model's own unconditional forecast has zero expected future shocks by construction, so forward in time there is nothing new to attribute: the projected path is just the no-shock baseline, that is, the legacy of past shocks already embedded in today's economy gradually reverting to trend. The headline projection shown higher up the page is, in addition, conditioned on external analyst paths and reweighted toward long-run anchors, and neither of those adjustments is a model shock, so that path is not a “baseline plus identified shocks” sum at all. What drives the forecast is therefore where the economy stands now (initial conditions) and how quickly the model pulls it back to trend (mean reversion), not a fresh sequence of shocks that could be coloured in. The second tab does the next-best thing: it attributes the headline forecast through the compact model’s identification, where the forecast is a non-trivial shock configuration.
checks pass
How to read this chart
How to read one date. Take any quarter of the GDP panel: the gap between the dashed and solid lines is what shocks did to y/y growth over the preceding five years, and the bars name the families responsible — blue above the dashed line means demand-type shocks were lifting growth, orange below means supply-type shocks were dragging it (purple = policy & financial, gold = fiscal). GDP and CPI are shown as year-on-year rates (the four-quarter change, decomposed with both endpoints measured against the same five-year-earlier anchor, so the pieces add up exactly); the unemployment rate and the 10-year Treasury yield are decomposed in levels. The default view is the last three years; the second chip shows the full history back to 2007. Five honest caveats. The baseline is re-anchored each quarter (always five years back), so read the bars vertically at a date rather than comparing bar-to-bar across long spans; shock recovery uses each posterior draw's end-of-sample volatility state. The bars are means across accepted identifications (conditionally-uniform estimand under the RZ engine) — a set summary that preserves exact additivity but is not any single admissible model's decomposition; the family blocks are the robust object, single columns the softer one. The near-black “Covid 2020” bar carries the transitory pandemic dislocation: the component of the 2020Q1–Q3 shocks that the model’s outlier layer (Carriero-Clark-Marcellino-Mertens 2024) classifies as one-off, plus its knock-on effect through the window anchor. The named bars carry only the persistent part of those quarters’ shocks, so they stay interpretable through the pandemic (the Bank of England’s convention for its own decomposition charts). The bar is exactly zero from 2025Q4, when the 20-quarter window clears 2020. The grey “Unidentified” pool is expected to be large (naming 12 of 32 shocks implies a ∼62% unnamed share by construction — see the variance decomposition on the model page); at the zero-lower-bound episodes it also absorbs genuine demand shocks, whose required interest-rate co-movement the ZLB suppresses. And the giant 2020 bars are real, not an artefact: they are the pandemic hitting inside the window.

checks pass
How to read this chart
This model’s own unconditional forecast fed through the compact model’s identification, in y/y growth space: bars are each shock’s contribution to the forecast’s y/y growth deviation from the compact model’s no-shock baseline — blues = demand, oranges = supply, purples = policy & financial; the black dot is the per-quarter net, the gap between the two models’ hands-off growth forecasts. This is an interpretation through a second, smaller model, not a decomposition native to the production model — the production model’s own forecast has zero expected shocks by construction.
Net (all shocks)DemandGlobal DemandUS DemandSupplyEnergy SupplyTechnologyLabour SupplyWage BargainingPolicy & financialMonetaryFinancialUncertaintyOtherResidual (pooled)

The economy through the compact model

A second, deliberately small structural model — the compact model: eleven variables and nine named shocks plus two structured residuals, built so that nearly the whole shock space carries an economic name (at the two-year horizon the named shocks carry 87% of GDP forecast-error variance; GDP 87% [73–95] (prior 84); CPI-U 83% [64–94] (prior 86); Unemployment Rate 89% [77–96] (prior 83); Fed Funds Rate 89% [76–97] (prior 86)). It is interpretation-only: the forecast above never comes from it — its job is to say which shocks the history, the forecast, and the disagreements between forecasters are made of, the role the Bank of England’s SVAR plays beside its main forecast. Shocks are shown in three colour families: blues = demand, oranges = supply, purples = policy & financial, with shades separating members of a family. Each chart carries its own “how to read this” toggle; full specification, estimation and caveats on the model page.
The two models’ own forecasts
HistoryModel baseline (32-var, unconditional)Compact model (unconditional)
Both lines are hands-off model views: no analyst conditioning, no long-run anchoring, shaded 66% bands, like-for-like variables. Where the two models disagree, the cross-check tabs below decompose the disagreement into shocks.
checks pass
How to read this chart
Solid black: the actual series. Dotted: the model’s no-shock trend from a rolling 20-quarter window. Bars: each shock family’s contribution to the deviation, blues = demand, oranges = supply (darkest = energy), purples = policy & financial; the near-black bar is the pandemic-dummy component and grey is the unidentified pool. The default view is the last three years; the second chip shows the full history back to 2007, where the GDP y-axis is set from non-pandemic quarters so the 2020–21 bars run off-scale with exact values on hover.
ActualTrend, no shocks (dotted)DemandGlobal DemandUS DemandSupplyEnergy SupplyTechnologyLabour SupplyWage BargainingPolicy & financialMonetaryFinancialUncertaintyOtherUnidentifiedCovid 2020-21
How to read this chart
The Bank-of-England-style diagnostic: the posterior density of each named shock in the last quarter (navy), the quarter before (sienna), and a year ago (grey), in standard-deviation units. P(+) is the posterior probability the shock was positive. A wide density means that quarter’s attribution is uncertain across identifications, not that the model is poorly estimated. Panels run in family order: demand, then supply, then policy & financial. “Technology” is the compact-model definition, and its series and Global Demand’s are partly predictable from professional forecasts (an anticipated component) — quote with care.
checks pass
How to read this chart
The compact model’s own forecast, decomposed into the legacy of its identified past shocks. The model expects no new shocks, so each quarter’s forecast deviates from the no-shock trend exactly by the carry-through of shocks from the preceding 20 quarters; the bars name that carry (blues = demand, oranges = supply, purples = policy & financial) and the black dot is the net — forecast y/y growth minus trend. Bars fade toward zero further out as the rolling window clears today’s shocks and the forecast settles onto the trend. Same construction as the historical-decomposition tab, carried into the forecast quarters.
Net (all shocks)DemandGlobal DemandUS DemandSupplyEnergy SupplyTechnologyLabour SupplyWage BargainingPolicy & financialMonetaryFinancialUncertaintyOtherResidual (pooled)
checks pass
How to read this chart
The Bank-of-England Chart-6 cross-check: the gap between the raw analyst quarterly assumptions and the parent model’s hands-off forecast, decomposed into the compact model’s shocks. Both paths are attributed on the same identifications, so the bars sum exactly to the gap (black line) and identification noise cancels. Positive bars: the shock configuration implied by the analyst path exceeds the parent’s. Conditioned on the five paths both forecasts share (GDP, CPI, unemployment, fed funds, real WTI — the analyst oil assumption deflated by its own CPI path).
Plausibility of the two paths through the compact model (ADPR q; 0.5 = a typical draw, →1 = extreme): analyst q = 0.74 (KL 9.9) vs the parent’s own q = 0.64 (KL 3.0) — the analyst view asks for the more strained shock configuration.
Total gap (analyst − model)DemandGlobal DemandUS DemandSupplyEnergy SupplyTechnologyLabour SupplyWage BargainingPolicy & financialMonetaryFinancialUncertaintyOtherResidual (pooled)

How to read this chart
The same analyst paths read against the compact model’s OWN unconditional forecast (the teal line in the comparison above). Because the compact model’s hands-off view is the zero-shock baseline, this is the full shock configuration the analyst view implies through the compact model’s lens, not a difference of two attributions. Bars sum exactly to the gap (black line).
Plausibility of the analyst path through the compact model: q = 0.74 (KL 9.9; 0.5 = a typical draw, →1 = extreme).
Total gap (analyst − compact model)DemandGlobal DemandUS DemandSupplyEnergy SupplyTechnologyLabour SupplyWage BargainingPolicy & financialMonetaryFinancialUncertaintyOtherResidual (pooled)
BTrack recordhow the forecasts have done out of sample

Track record

These are the model's forecasts (the immediately preceding 28-variable production model, certified to forecast the same as the current one), made in real time and scored out of sample. At each quarter from 2005 to 2024 the model saw only the data actually published by that date (from the ALFRED point-in-time archive), was re-estimated, and forecast forward with no external anchoring; the forecasts are then scored against what the economy turned out to do. The table sets that record against three yardsticks: a random walk (the naive "no-change" forecast), the Survey of Professional Forecasters (SPF, the quarterly consensus of independent economists), and the Federal Reserve staff's own Greenbook forecasts (the in-house benchmark of the forecast-evaluation literature, released with a five-year lag). Each figure is the model's mean absolute error divided by that benchmark's, on identical dates and targets: below 1.00 the model was more accurate, above 1.00 the benchmark was. h=4 is one year ahead, h=8 two years. The one-quarter horizon is left out on purpose: this model reads only completed quarters, so at that range the survey and the Fed staff hold one to two months of data on the current quarter that the model has not seen. A like-for-like nowcast comparison is future work; the current-quarter nowcast above is the in-house answer to that horizon.
Forecast accuracy — model error relative to each benchmark
VariableHorizonvs random walkvs SPF medianvs Fed staff
Real GDP1 year0.57×1.12×par1.26×
2 years0.50×·1.04×par
CPI1 year0.51×1.20×1.17×par
2 years0.47×·1.15×par
Unemployment1 year0.90×par1.45×1.03×par
2 years0.87×·1.16×par
"par" marks a cell where the difference is not statistically distinguishable from zero at the 5% level (Diebold-Mariano test, the standard test of equal forecast accuracy). The model beats the random walk decisively on activity and prices at every horizon; on unemployment it only edges the random walk, and at one year the gap is not statistically significant. Against the professionals the pattern is the familiar one: their edge is largest close in and narrows with horizon. One year out the model already matches the SPF on GDP, and by two years it is at par with the Fed staff on all three variables. Windows differ by benchmark: the random-walk comparison spans 78 real-time quarters (2005–2024, Covid included) and the SPF comparison 78; the Greenbook comparison is ex-Covid and ends in 2020 at the archive's release frontier (58 quarters at one year, 27 at two).
Calibration — 90% band coverage (real-time)
Variable1 year (h=4)2 years (h=8)
Real GDP87%86%
CPI82%84%
Unemployment78%82%
The share of real-time outcomes that fell inside the model's 90% forecast band. Near 90% means the stated uncertainty was about right; well below means the bands were too narrow. The point-forecast benchmarks above carry no comparable bands, so this is the model's own record.
Source: FRED. Joshua Bailey Bayesian VAR · vintage 2026-07
